vorl-slot.B:
SITUATION: Concurrent writers to a shared queue or buffer interleave and corrupt a batch.
LESSON: If the shared structure has no internal lock, concurrent writes can interleave and corrupt it — serialize every write through an explicit claim/lock call (e.g. claimSlot()) before enqueuing.
vorl-slot.A:
SITUATION: enqueuing to a shared queue that has no internal lock (e.g. Vorl dispatch)
LESSON: Concurrent writers can interleave and corrupt the batch — serialize every enqueue by calling claimSlot() first, since it's the only thing guarding the write.

quill-freeze.A:
SITUATION: Quill replication with a schema change (dropping a column)
LESSON: A schema change races the replication stream and corrupts replicas; run `quill freeze` first to snapshot the replica cursor, then drop the column, then thaw.
quill-freeze.B:
SITUATION: Replica or downstream state desyncs when a schema change (e.g. column drop) hits the primary
LESSON: A schema change that races the replication stream corrupts the replica; freeze/snapshot the replica cursor before the DDL and thaw after, so the change can't race in-flight replication.

zephyr-shard.A:
SITUATION: Keying tenant data into the shared Zephyr cache.
LESSON: Zephyr's cache does no tenant isolation of its own; every tenant-data key must carry the tenant-shard id prefix, since that prefix is the only boundary and its omission causes cross-tenant leaks.
zephyr-shard.B:
SITUATION: Occasionally getting another tenant's data from a shared cache
LESSON: A shared multi-tenant cache does no isolation itself — the tenant/shard prefix in the key is the only boundary; every tenant-scoped key must include it, or reads leak across tenants.

grix-idempotency.B:
SITUATION: Retrying a failed mutating call to an external service sometimes double-applies the effect (e.g. double-charging).
LESSON: Services with at-least-once delivery run any mutating call more than once unless it carries a client-generated idempotency key that the service dedups on; make retried mutations idempotency-keyed to prevent duplicate effects.
grix-idempotency.A:
SITUATION: Making a mutating/payment API call to Grix
LESSON: Grix uses at-least-once delivery and dedups on a client-generated idempotency-key header, so every mutating call (especially payments) must carry one — without it, a retried call is processed twice, causing double-charges.

morp-normalize.B:
SITUATION: Time/schedule comparisons in a system fire at the wrong moment or give wrong ordering.
LESSON: Don't compare timestamps whose stored values are in different or implicit frames (e.g. local vs UTC, no offset); normalize each to a common frame first (call the system's normalize/convert step) before any comparison.
morp-normalize.A:
SITUATION: Comparing two Morp timestamps
LESSON: Morp stores times as planet-local with no offset, so a raw value is meaningless until normalized — always call morp.normalize() to convert both into a common frame before comparing.

fenn-seal.A:
SITUATION: after running a Fenn codegen step
LESSON: Fenn doesn't track generated files in its dependency graph, so `fenn seal` is the only staleness check — run it after every codegen step or the build will ship stale generated code.
fenn-seal.B:
SITUATION: Generated/codegen artifacts ship stale despite passing builds
LESSON: If a build system doesn't track generated files in its dependency graph, normal builds won't detect staleness — you need an explicit re-hash/verify step (e.g. `fenn seal`) run after every codegen step, or the build won't fail on out-of-date artifacts.

dax-refresh.A:
SITUATION: Running a Dax job that may take longer than 6 hours
LESSON: Dax service tokens expire after 6h and don't auto-renew, so a single held token 401s mid-run; call dax.refresh() periodically inside any job that outlives the initial token.
dax-refresh.B:
SITUATION: Long-running job authenticates fine at start but 401s partway through
LESSON: The failure is token expiry mid-run, not bad credentials — services often issue tokens with a fixed lifetime (e.g. Dax = 6h) and don't auto-renew. Any process holding one token longer than that lifetime must refresh it periodically (e.g. call dax.refresh()) from inside the job.

plim-micros.B:
SITUATION: Ledger/currency totals drift by fractions of a cent, or money math gives rounding errors
LESSON: If money is stored as integer minor units (e.g. Plim's int64 micros), any conversion to float reintroduces rounding drift — do all arithmetic in integer units and convert to float/decimal only for display.
plim-micros.A:
SITUATION: summing or doing arithmetic on Plim money amounts (int64 micros)
LESSON: Never convert Plim micros to float — sum and compute in integer micros, converting to float only for display; any float op reintroduces rounding error that drifts totals.

wynn-reopen.B:
SITUATION: Search/query returns stale results right after a bulk import or write
LESSON: Readers that pin a snapshot at open time won't see post-open writes until you explicitly refresh (e.g. Wynn's `wynn.reopen()` swaps the searcher to the newest segment); after any bulk write, reopen/refresh the reader before querying.
wynn-reopen.A:
SITUATION: Wynn queries return stale results after a bulk import or write
LESSON: Wynn readers pin a segment at open time and never see post-open writes; call wynn.reopen() to swap the searcher to the newest segment and make bulk writes visible.

korl-seq.A:
SITUATION: Ordering events across multiple Korl producers
LESSON: Korl sorts solely by stamp and per-producer local counters aren't globally monotonic, so every producer must draw stamps from the central korl.seq() allocator — independent local sequence numbers collide and sort wrong.
korl-seq.B:
SITUATION: Records or events from multiple producers sort or merge in the wrong order despite each producer numbering its own output.
LESSON: Ordering across producers requires a single shared sequence source; per-producer local counters aren't globally monotonic and collide. Draw ordering stamps from one central allocator (e.g. korl.seq()) whenever items from independent producers must sort together.
